1. Theory

1.1. Convex Sets

Convex Set
Definition 1.1.1
Let . is convex iff

Intuitively, this is a set which contains no “gaps”, i.e empty regions between points in the set.

It’s straightforward to show convex sets remain convex under a variety of transforms, such as Cartesian products and intersections. Here are some other useful convexity preserving transformations.

Theorem 1.1.1
An affine function applied to a convex set is also a convex set.

Proof: Suppose . Is ? Well, we know each point must have at least one pre-image in . Call these and respectively. Furthermore, the convex combination of these points must also be in . Finally, we have

So has a pre-image in , and the claim is shown. You can also show this for affine inverses.

Theorem 1.1.2
Perspective functions preserve convexity. Note that perspective functions require the last coordinate to be strictly positive.

Proof: Does ? Let the pre-images of and be as follows.

Composing affine and projective functions lets you say linear fractional functions preserve convexity.

1.2. Topology

Interior Point
A point such that a centered ball of some sufficiently small radius epsilon fits entirely within .
Relative Interior Point
An slight refinement of an interior point. Instead of saying all points in the ball must fit within , you say all points in the ball that are also on the affine hull of must fit within . This lets you define interiors for subspaces.
Boundary Point
A point such that a centered ball always intersects and ‘s complement. Alternatively, a point such that all centered balls of radius less than some threshold intersects and ‘s complement.
Open Set
I don’t contain my boundary.
Closed Set
I contain my boundary.
Compact Set
I contain my boundary and I have a bounded size.

1.3. Cones

A convex conic set can be used to define an ordering as follows.

A cool convex conic set is the set of positive definite matrices.

Intuitively,

Not every set of matrices can be ordered under this metric (e.g. neither A - B or B - A is PD), which is why cones are said to be a generalization of inequalities - they forgo universal ordering.

Polar Cone
Definition 1.3.2
Let be any set. Then the polar cone .

Intuitively, this is the set of vectors which make more than a degree angle to all vectors in .

Theorem 1.3.3
Polar cones are convex cones.

Proof:

Normal Cone
Definition 1.3.3

Intuitively, this cone is all the vectors which are more than degrees off from any direction into the set. So for interior points, they’re empty, for flat boundaries they’re rays, and for corners, they’re a proper cone. You can verify this is indeed a convex cone.

Tangent Cone
Definition 1.3.4
is the set of directions we can move in which keep us in the set.

It’s easy to see the Tangent Cone is just the Polar Cone of the Normal Cone.

The above two cones are useful for quantifying vertices, edges, and if we’ve reached optimality.

1.4. Convex Functions

Convex Function
Definition 1.4.5

A function is convex if its domain is convex, and

This object is the heart of convex analysis. Intuitively, the above definition says convex functions are functions where a line between two points on its graph lie above the rest of the graph. However, there are many other useful and equivalent ways to characterize them.

1st Order Convexity Statement
Theorem 1.4.4

This essentially says any tangent line lower-bounds the convex function. In the first diagram we can see how the zeroth order condition implies an upper bound on the slope (), and in the second diagram we see how above the line draw by the zeroth order condition, no lower bound will be beneath both boundary points ().

2nd Order Convexity Statement
Theorem 1.4.5

The second statement says the Hessian must be positive semi-definite. This can be interpreted as saying no matter what direction I walk in, the slope along that curve must be non-decreasing.

The first diagram shows the , as otherwise one of the lower bounds is broken. The second diagram shows as no matter which direction you move the function curves up away from your tangent line.

A useful tool for relating convex sets to convex functions is the epigraph.

Epigraph
Definition 1.4.6

Theorem 1.4.6
is convex iff is convex.

Proof: Suppose . If is convex, then by definition of convexity,

By definition of an epigraph, this means

Thus, is a convex function. The reverse direction is similar.

From this property, you can easily deduce that maxes of convex function are still convex, and a lot of other properties. Here’s an epigraph-based proof for a problem that will show up later.

Convex Partial Minimization
Theorem 1.4.7

Consider the partial minimization of over the set :

If is always finite, is convex.

One example of this problem is finding the closest point in a convex set to a given point.

Proof: First observe

Now, consider the intersection of with

is convex, as it can be written . is convex by the theorem above and the cartesian product of convex sets is also convex.

Furthermore,

Now, we can use an affine function to drop the term. Affine functions preserve convexity.

Now consider . This can be written

Hence, is convex which implies is convex.

2.1. Gradient Descent

Gradient Descent
Definition 2.1.6
Descent Lemma
Lemma 2.1.1

For a -smooth function, gradient descent with yields

Let’s see what -smoothness tells us about GD.

Picture a circle of small radius (no bigger then ) around . Within this small region of trust () all vectors in the circle point at least somewhat in the direction of . If you imagine breaking the path from up, each step in that path will decrease the function. This is exactly what the proof exploits.

Proof:

Theorem 2.1.1

For a -smooth function, gradient descent gives the following guarantee

Proof: Suppose it was false. Then, the gradient must exceed this inequality on every step.

Ah, but the gradient at the global optimum is zero, so we’ve reached a contradiction.

Theorem 2.1.2

For a convex -smooth function, gradient descent with yields

Without convexity, we could only say that the slope got pretty small. The reason we can say more with convexity is it gives a lower-bound on the norm of the gradient which by the descent lemma means we make substantial progress per step. This would not be the case if you were in a relatively flat region of a non-convex curve (shown above).

Proof:

Substituting this lower-bound into the descent lemma yields…

This is a finite-difference equation! You can rearrange it to make it solvable with telescoping.

So we get

This bound is not as tight as the theorem, however this proof is direct.

Theorem 2.1.3

If is -smooth and -strongly convex, yields

That, is gradient descent yields linear convergence.

Proof:

Again, a slightly worse result then it is possible to prove.

This term is interesting. It’s called the condition number of the problem. The intuitive reason for what this represents is the maximum difference in curvature between different directions. If you have a fixed step size, you have to make your step size small enough to deal with the maximum curvature regions. However, this means you move very slowly along the minimum curvature regions (shown above). Dealing with this problem is why momentum and higher-order methods were developed!

2.2. Subgradient Methods

Subgradient
Definition 2.2.7

From the definition, it’s also immediately clear the subgradient is a convex set. If the subgradient is non-empty everywhere the function is convex by a relaxed version of the first order convexity criteria. If is differentiable, the subgradient has at most one element (e.g. there’s only one “tangent” line). Notice that unlike the gradient, the subgradient is a global property, it is a constraint on the entire function.

It’s easy to see the following properties.



One interesting property is the sub-gradient of the maximum of functions.

This is pretty intuitive, the functions which don’t achieve the maximum don’t define the curvature, and the convex hull essentially means anything in between the two subgradients is valid (which you can justify by looking at the green region in the above diagram).

Here’s an example application. Consider . What’s the subgradient at ? Well, using the above property on , we immediately obtain the convex hull of which is .

We can generalize our optimality conditions from earlier to use subgradients.

Lemma 2.2.2
Let . For , the subgradient is .

Proof:

Theorem 2.2.4
is optimal if

Proof:

must be in the subgradient of for it to be optimal.

Hence which shows the claim.

Subgradient Method
Definition 2.2.8
Essentially, replace the gradient in gradient descent with the subgradient.

Interestingly, the negative subgradient is not guaranteed to be a descent direction! You can see this with . Furthermore, this can happen even at a suboptimal point. Consider the function . At a valid negative subgradient is . Hence, . You can see some nice examples here.

However, for 1D functions this actually cannot happen. This is because the subgradient is convex and for a point to be suboptimal it must not contain zero. Hence, the subgradient and the true descent direction always have the same sign in this case.

Theorem 2.2.5

For a convex -lipschitz function and step sizes , we have

Proof: Expand in terms of to obtain.

Now apply the -lipschitz property.

Performing a telescoping sum yields the desired claim.

To extract a rate out of this, let . Then we have

Let’s assume . Then we get

Set and .

So the rate ends up being a bit worse than with -smoothness. That’s because we were forced to take smaller and smaller steps to deal with the inadequacy of our linear model. -smooth functions give curvature information and can guarantee a substantive decrease per step.

2.2.1. Interior Points

The point of the Subgradient method is it can be applied to non-differentiable convex functions. Here’s an important example. Consider the problem of finding a point at the intersection of some number of convex sets.

This is a very useful problem because oftentimes we are minimizing a function over a constrained domain. For convex minimization, if you can find a single point in this intersection, then following the gradient while staying in the convex set is sufficient to find the optimal answer (this approach is known as interior point methods).

We can rewrite this problem into a convex minimization problem. Define

This function tells us to find the closest point for each set, and report the distance to the furthest point. The minimum of zero will occur if we’re in an intersection of all sets. This function is clearly convex as it is the maximum of convex minimization problems (and we’ve shown both operations preserve convexity).

The max makes this non-differentiable, but we can still solve this with Subgradient methods. In particular, the Subgradient of this function will tell us we should move directly towards one of the convex sets (imagine we just had one convex set, and then you can use the property about the max of Subgradients).

I won’t show this, but you can prove that this algorithm remains stable if you move all the way to the projection of the current point onto that set (see the above diagram). Pretty elegant algorithm!

2.3. Projected Gradient Descent

Projected Gradient Descent
Definition 2.3.9

The algorithm is quite simple, simply take gradient steps, and then force your point into the convex set after each step.

This is sensible because projection onto convex sets has some nice properties.

Lemma 2.3.3

Proof: We know a point is the minimizer of a convex function if the negative gradient is more than ninety degrees off from any direction into the set (there is no direction we can move which decreases the function). Since minimizing the is the same as minimizing we have

Theorem 2.3.6

The proof is just formalizing the above geometric argument.

Proof:

This is a super useful property! Observe that this means projection strictly improves the answer.

Using this, it’s easy to show all the rates we showed for earlier methods immediately generalize to projected gradient descent.

2.4. Proximal Gradient Descent

Proximal Gradient Descent
Definition 2.4.10

Minimize where are convex, but only is guaranteed differentiable.

This can be seen as a generalization of both gradient descent and projected gradient descent by choosing to be zero or the indicator function. The main point of this is that just like projected gradient descent recovered the rates of gradient descent, we can recover those rates here too. This is an improvement over the rate you would get by applying the subgradient method. The idea behind is a step of GD can be viewed as minimizing a quadratic, and now we’re just minimizing a quadratic plus an auxiliary term.

Theorem 2.4.7
is a contraction.

Proof:

Hence, is a minimizer if and only if

You can then apply subgradient monotonicity

Generalized Gradient
Definition 2.4.11
Theorem 2.4.8

Proof:

So, you can see this approach takes a qualitatively different step then the subgradient method, it evaluates the sub-differential at an offset point. This will actually lead to a better rate of convergence!
